title: Flight Routes Check
description:
There are n cities and m flight connections. Your task is to check if you can travel from any city to any other city using the available flights.

Input:
- First line: two integers n and m — the number of cities and flights. Cities are numbered 1..n.
- Next m lines: two integers a and b — there is a one-way flight from a to b.

Output:
- Print "YES" if every city can reach every other city using the flights.
- Otherwise print "NO" and on the next line print two cities a and b such that you cannot travel from a to b. If there are several answers, print any.

Time limit: 1.00 s
Memory limit: 512 MB

examples.explanation: From city 4 you cannot reach city 2.

mcqChain.questionText: Suppose there are 3 cities and flights: 1 → 2, 2 → 3. Is it guaranteed that you can travel from 3 to 1?

options.label: No, because flights are one-way and there is no path from 3 back to 1.
Yes, because if 1 can reach 3, then 3 can reach 1.
Yes, because you can always reverse the route.
It depends on n and m only, not the specific flights.

mcqChain.explanation: One-way flights do not imply return paths. 3 has no outgoing path to 1 here.

wrongExplanations.1: Reachability is not symmetric in directed flights.
wrongExplanations.2: You cannot reverse a one-way flight unless a reverse flight exists.

mcqChain.hint: Think about direction: does anything go out of 3 toward 1?

mcqChain.questionText: For n=2 and flights: 1 → 2 only. What should the program output?
mcqChain.explanation: You cannot travel from 2 to 1, so print NO and a pair (2,1).

wrongExplanations.1: There is a path 1→2; the failure is from 2 to 1, not 1 to 2.

mcqChain.hint: Find a pair (a,b) where there is no path a→b.

mcqChain.questionText: For flights: 1 → 2, 2 → 3, 3 → 1. What should the program output?
mcqChain.explanation: From any city you can reach the others via the cycle, so output YES.

wrongExplanations.1: There is a path between every ordered pair here.

mcqChain.hint: Check each ordered pair using the cycle.

mcqChain.questionText: If from city 1 you can reach all cities, is it enough to conclude the answer is YES?

options.label: Yes, because reaching all from 1 implies all pairs are connected.
No, because some city might not be able to reach another (e.g., back to 1).
Yes, if there are no self-loops.

mcqChain.explanation: Reaching all from 1 doesn't ensure others can reach back. We must also check who can reach 1.

wrongExplanations.0: Directed reachability is not symmetric; more checking is needed.

mcqChain.hint: Consider whether some city can reach 1 even if 1 reaches it.

mcqChain.questionText: A naive idea: from every city, run a traversal along outgoing flights to see if it reaches all others. What's the time cost in terms of n and m?
mcqChain.explanation: Each traversal is O(n+m). Doing it from n cities costs O(n*(n+m)).

wrongExplanations.0: That's the cost of a single traversal, not n traversals.

mcqChain.hint: Multiply the cost of one full traversal by how many times you do it.

mcqChain.questionText: Pick a start city s (say s=1). After traversing along outgoing flights from s, you find a city t was not visited. Which pair immediately proves the answer is NO?
mcqChain.explanation: t is not reachable from s, so (s,t) witnesses failure.

wrongExplanations.1: We discovered s cannot reach t, not the other way around.

mcqChain.hint: Which direction did you try to travel in that failed?

mcqChain.questionText: If all cities are visited from s along outgoing flights, what else must be checked to conclude YES?

options.label: That every city can reach s using the given flights.
That the graph has at least n−1 flights.

mcqChain.explanation: We must ensure every city can also travel to s; then any a→b can go a→…→s→…→b.

wrongExplanations.0: Reaching all from s doesn't imply all can reach s in directed flights.

mcqChain.hint: Think in terms of paths ending at s.

mcqChain.questionText: How can we efficiently find all cities that can travel to s using only the given directed flights?

options.label: Traverse starting at s, but move from x to y only if there is a flight y → x in the input.
Traverse from each city to see if it can reach s, aggregating results.
Traverse again from s along outgoing flights.
Sort flights and scan linearly for paths to s.

mcqChain.explanation: Following edges in the opposite direction from s visits exactly the cities that can reach s.

wrongExplanations.1: That repeats work n times; we want one more traversal, not n.
wrongExplanations.2: That tells who s can reach, not who can reach s.

mcqChain.hint: Use the same traversal idea but consume edges pointing into your current city.

mcqChain.questionText: During this "incoming-edges" traversal from s, a city v is never visited. Which pair shows failure?
mcqChain.explanation: If v is not visited when moving via incoming edges, v cannot reach s. So (v, s) proves failure.

wrongExplanations.0: This phase checks who can reach s, not who s can reach.

mcqChain.hint: Which direction fails in this second check?

mcqChain.questionText: Why do the two traversals (outgoing from s, and incoming to s) together guarantee the full requirement?
mcqChain.diagram: Paths: a ...-> s and s ...-> b

options.label: Because if s reaches everyone and everyone reaches s, then any a can go to b via s.
Because two traversals cover all edges twice.
Because visiting s twice is sufficient.
Because the flights are undirected in effect.

mcqChain.explanation: Any a→b path can be formed by a→…→s then s→…→b if both properties hold.

wrongExplanations.1: Edge coverage count doesn't imply pairwise reachability.

mcqChain.hint: Combine a path from a to s and a path from s to b.

mcqChain.questionText: Which city can we choose as the start s for these two traversals?

options.label: Only a city with the most outgoing flights.
Only a city with the fewest incoming flights.
Only a city that is already known to reach all others.

mcqChain.explanation: Any fixed city works; choosing 1 is convenient for implementation.

wrongExplanations.1: The choice does not depend on degrees.

mcqChain.hint: You can fix any index as s.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider n=4 with flights: 1→2, 2→3, 3→1, and 1→4. From s=1, what happens?

options.label: First traversal visits all; second traversal misses some cities.
First traversal misses some cities; second traversal visits all.

mcqChain.explanation: 1 reaches all (1,2,3,4). But not all can reach 1 (e.g., 4 cannot), so second traversal misses 4.
mcqChain.hint: Check who can reach 1 using the given flights.

mcqChain.questionText: Choose a robust input-reading setup in Python for large inputs and start a main function.

options.label:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.readline().split()
    it = iter(data)

mcqChain.explanation: Reading all tokens at once is fast and avoids per-line overhead; we also guard empty input.

wrongExplanations.1: Reading line-by-line can pass, but options later will expect token iterator and empty-input guard.
wrongExplanations.3: Not converting to ints yet and no iterator/guard; also input() may be too slow for worst cases.

mcqChain.hint: Use sys.stdin.read and tokenize once.

mcqChain.questionText: Parse n, m; build adjacency lists for flights and for traversing via incoming edges.

options.label:
n = int(next(it)); m = int(next(it))
    adj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    radj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    for _ in range(m):
        a = int(next(it)); b = int(next(it))
        adj[a].append(b)
        radj[b].append(a)

n = int(next(it)); m = int(next(it))
    adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]
    radj = [[] for _ in range(n)]
    for _ in range(m):
        a = int(next(it)); b = int(next(it))
        adj[a-1].append(b-1)
        radj[a-1].append(b-1)

n = int(next(it)); m = int(next(it))
    adj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    radj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    for _ in range(m):
        a = int(next(it)); b = int(next(it))
        adj[b].append(a)
        radj[a].append(b)

n = int(next(it)); m = int(next(it))
    adj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    radj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    for _ in range(m):
        a = next(it); b = next(it)
        adj[int(a)].append(int(b))
        radj[int(a)].append(int(b))

mcqChain.explanation: We keep 1-based indexing and store outgoing edges in adj and incoming traversal neighbors in radj via radj[b].append(a).

wrongExplanations.1: Mixing 0-based with later 1-based loops and building radj in the same direction is inconsistent.
wrongExplanations.2: Swapping adj and radj makes both traversals wrong.

mcqChain.hint: radj should let you move from x to y if y→x exists.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
n = int(next(it)); m = int(next(it))
    adj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    radj = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]
    for _ in range(m):
        a = int(next(it)); b = int(next(it))
        adj[a].append(b)
        radj[b].append(a)

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize the first traversal from start city s=1.

options.label:
start = 1
    visited = [False]*(n+1)
    stack = []
    # don't mark visited until pop

mcqChain.explanation: We need size n+1 for 1..n and should mark start visited and push it to begin traversal.

wrongExplanations.1: visited size n misses index n and mismatches 1-based indexing.
wrongExplanations.3: Marking all True prevents any traversal.

mcqChain.hint: Use 1-based indexing consistently.

mcqChain.questionText: Fill the loop to explore all cities reachable from start via outgoing flights.
mcqChain.explanation: Standard stack-based propagation marking when first discovered avoids duplicates and terminates correctly.

wrongExplanations.2: Blindly marking neighbors ignores reachability from start and initial visited state.
wrongExplanations.3: Infinite loop if stack becomes empty; also no visited checks causes duplicates.

mcqChain.hint: Pop, iterate neighbors, push unvisited neighbors, and mark them.

mcqChain.questionText: After the first traversal, check for any unvisited city and print the required failure pair.

options.label:
if visited.count(False) == 0:
        print("NO")
        print(1, start)
        return

mcqChain.explanation: Scan 1..n for the first unvisited i and report (start, i) as the failing direction.

wrongExplanations.1: Mixes 0-based and 1-based indices and reverses the pair direction.

mcqChain.hint: Report the first city not reached from start as (start, i).

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize the second traversal to find who can reach start using incoming edges.
mcqChain.explanation: Use a fresh visited array, push start, and mark it visited to begin the second traversal.

wrongExplanations.1: Reusing visited mixes the two phases and hides missing reachability to start.
wrongExplanations.3: You need to seed the traversal with start.

mcqChain.hint: It's a new traversal with its own visited state.

mcqChain.questionText: Fill the loop to explore all cities that can reach start by moving along incoming edges (using radj).
mcqChain.explanation: Move through radj to follow incoming edges; mark on first discovery and push.

wrongExplanations.1: Using adj again repeats the first traversal, not the incoming-edges traversal.
wrongExplanations.2: Without pushing, you won't propagate beyond immediate neighbors.

mcqChain.hint: Use the list that encodes neighbors y where y→u exists.

mcqChain.questionText: After the second traversal, check for any city that cannot reach start and finish the program.
mcqChain.explanation: Scan for the first city that cannot reach start and print (i, start); otherwise print YES and exit.

wrongExplanations.1: Condition and pair direction are wrong; we want cities that were NOT visited in second pass.

mcqChain.hint: This time report (i, start) for any unvisited i.

mcqChain.questionText: Trace the program on n=4 with flights: 1→2, 2→3, 3→1, 1→4. What does it print?
mcqChain.explanation: First pass reaches all. Second pass from 1 via incoming edges cannot reach 4; first missing is 2 or 4? Only 1 has incoming from 3; 2 and 3 are visited via incoming chain, but 4 cannot reach 1, so print NO and (4,1).

wrongExplanations.2: 2 can reach 1 (2→3→1). The failing city is 4.

mcqChain.hint: Who has a path to 1 using directed flights?

mcqChain.questionText: Trace the program on n=3 with a single flight: 2→3. Starting from s=1, what does it print?
mcqChain.explanation: First pass from 1 visits only 1. The first unvisited is 2, so it prints NO and (1,2).

wrongExplanations.3: We fail in the first pass already; we don't reach the second pass.

mcqChain.hint: In the first pass from 1, which is the first city not visited?

mcqChain.questionText: For n=2 with flights 1→2 and 2→1, what does the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: Both passes cover both cities, so the result is YES.
mcqChain.hint: Check both directions between 1 and 2.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of this solution in terms of n and m?
mcqChain.explanation: Each traversal visits nodes/edges at most once, and we do it twice: O(n+m).

wrongExplanations.1: We do not traverse from every city; only two traversals.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many times each edge is processed.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the extra space usage of the algorithm?

options.label: O(n + m) for adjacency structures and O(n) for visited arrays

mcqChain.explanation: We store adjacency lists (O(n+m)) and two visited arrays (O(n)).

wrongExplanations.1: We store the full graph, not constant memory.

mcqChain.hint: Consider both adj, radj and visited arrays.

mcqChain.questionText: Edge case: n=1, m=0. What should the program output?
mcqChain.explanation: With one city, it trivially reaches itself, so print YES.

wrongExplanations.1: The requirement is satisfied vacuously for a single city.

mcqChain.hint: Can the only city reach itself under the problem statement?

reasoningChain.question: Why is it not enough to know that one city reaches another in one direction for directed flights?
reasoningChain.insight: Directed edges are one-way; reachability is not symmetric.

reasoningChain.question: How can we quickly detect failure using one traversal from a fixed start city?
reasoningChain.insight: Traverse along outgoing flights from a fixed start (e.g., 1); if any city is not visited, report (start, that city).

reasoningChain.question: If the first traversal reaches all cities, what additional property must hold?
reasoningChain.insight: Every city must also be able to travel to the start; check who can reach start by moving via incoming edges.

reasoningChain.question: How do we implement the second check efficiently with the given flights?
reasoningChain.insight: Build neighbors for moving along incoming edges (for each a→b, add b→a in a separate list) and traverse from start.

reasoningChain.question: What failure pair should be reported after the second check if some city cannot reach start?
reasoningChain.insight: If a city v is not visited in the incoming-edges traversal, report (v, start).

reasoningChain.question: Why do these two traversals together imply all-pairs reachability?
reasoningChain.insight: If start reaches everyone and everyone reaches start, then any a can go to b via a→…→start→…→b.

reasoningChain.question: What are the performance guarantees of this approach?
reasoningChain.insight: Each edge and node is processed a constant number of times across two traversals: O(n+m) time and O(n+m) space.
